auth_prerequisites: Configure an LLM provider on first use with `goose configure` or the first-run wizard.
If keyring access is unavailable, provider credentials can be supplied through environment variables or config-backed secrets storage.
For local/offline operation, start a supported local provider such as Ollama or LM Studio before selecting it in `goose configure`.

non_interactive_execution.summary: Official docs describe `goose run` as a one-shot task runner that accepts inline text, instruction files, or stdin, exits automatically when complete, and can emit JSON for CI/CD automation.

offline_strategy.summary: Official provider docs say Goose supports local providers, keeps data private with local LLMs, and can work entirely offline when paired with supported local model backends.

observable_cli_surface.summary: Official docs expose debug output, JSON and stream-JSON modes, and no-session execution paths that make Goose observable enough for wrapper capture and automated evaluation.

redaction_fit.summary: Goose separates provider credentials from prompt text through provider configuration, environment variables, and secret storage, and the open-source repo makes the CLI surface inspectable for wrapper-side redaction policies.
redaction_fit.notes: This is an inference from the documented config/auth surfaces rather than a repo-owned probe of emitted stderr/stdout.

crate_first_fit.summary: Goose has a standalone public CLI install surface, active GitHub releases, and stable task-oriented commands that look suitable for a crate-first wrapper lane.
crate_first_fit.notes: The wrapper should prefer the shell installer or Homebrew over the archived PyPI package.

reproducibility.summary: Official docs document non-interactive install (`CONFIGURE=false`) and a pinned-version CI/CD flow, which is enough to reproduce CLI installation independently of interactive first-run setup.
reproducibility.notes: The PyPI evidence is weaker than the active docs/GitHub release train because the package is archived.

future_leverage.summary: Goose appears to have leverage beyond a narrow chat wrapper because the project is open source, extensible, and explicitly documents local providers plus MCP/ACP-oriented surfaces.

normalized_caveats: The `goose-ai` PyPI package is archived at version `0.9.11` while the active CLI release train on GitHub/docs is much newer; prefer the documented shell installer or Homebrew for onboarding.
Provider configuration is still required before useful execution unless install/config is pre-seeded.
Local-model operation exists, but some models lack tool-calling and require reduced extension usage.

evidence.title: Install goose | goose
evidence.excerpt: Install docs show the shell installer, support `CONFIGURE=false` for non-interactive setup, and note that first use prompts for provider configuration.

evidence.title: Running Tasks | goose
evidence.excerpt: The `goose run` guide documents inline text, instruction files, stdin, `--no-session`, debug output, and `json` or `stream-json` output for automation.

evidence.title: Configure LLM Provider | goose
evidence.excerpt: Provider docs say local LLMs keep data private, can work entirely offline, and may use env vars, keychain, or `secrets.yaml` for credentials.

evidence.title: GitHub - aaif-goose/goose
evidence.excerpt: GitHub describes Goose as an open-source, extensible AI agent, lists Apache-2.0, and shows latest release `v1.33.1` on 2026-04-29.

evidence.title: goose-ai · PyPI
evidence.excerpt: PyPI shows `goose-ai 0.9.11`, Python `>=3.10`, and explicitly marks the package archived with no new releases expected.
